Fern Polrrrcal PRACTTcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
August 17,2006
Jeffrey G. Scott
General Counsel
Grossmont Healthcare District
16935 West Bernardo Drive, Suite 170
San Diego, CA 92127
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-06-148
Dear Mr. Scott:
This letter is in response to your request for advice, on behalf of Mr. Robert p.
Yarris a board member of the Grossmont Healthcare District ("Healthcare District,),
regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the ,,Act,).i
Because your request seeks general guidance beyond a specific goverrmental decision,
we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.t
QUESTION
Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit Mr. Yarris from making,
participating in making, or influencing Healthcare District decisions related to a Health
Education Center, which may be constructed by the Grossmont-Cuyamaca Community
College District ("College District'), considering his emplo5rment with the Grossmont-
Cuyamaca Community College District Auxiliary Or ganiz ation ("College Auxiliary,,)?
CONCLUSION
Mr. Yarris will have a conflict of interest disqualifizing him from making,
participating in making, or influencing Healthcare District decisions related to the Health
Education Center if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
I Government Code sections 8 1000 - gl}14. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not p.oiria" the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83114; rggulation 18329(c)(3).)
rrygl


File No. I-06-148
Page No. 2
effect on the College Auxiliary, if the nexus test is met, or if there is a personal financial
effect on Mr. Yarris as discussed herein.
FACTS
The Healthcare District is located in La Mesa, Califomia and is organizedunder
the provisions of the Health and Safety Code sections 32000 et seq., to provide healthcare
services and to operate healthcare related facilities for residents iniast San Diego
County.
Mr. Yarris has served on the Healthcare District Board since December 1992. In
addition to his Healthcare District Board position, Mr. Yarris is also an employee of the
College Auxiliary, holding a position as the Program Director of the Regional'Health
Occupations Resource Center. As an employee of the College Auxiliary, Mr. yarris
teaches a class at Grossmont College two to three times a week and provides leadership
and direction to regional community colleges in workforce development. In addition,
Mr. Yarris's work involves the administration and management of all grants and budgets
for the College Auxiliary.
The College Auxiliary is a nonprofit public corporation, which receives grants
from private foundations and contracts with the College District. As an employee of the
College Auxiliary, Mr. Yarris receives no salary or other form of remuneration from the
College District.
The College District and the Grossmont College, which is a college of the College
District, are exploring the construction of a Health Education Center on property o*r"d
by the Healthcare District, which may also include funding from the UeAtfrcare District
for nursing and other healthcare-related education programs.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from makin g, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental deJision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest, in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic interests. (Section 87l}3;regulation 1g700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eighrstep standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualifyingconflict of interest in a given goveflrmental decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regulation 18700(bXl)-(S).)


File No. I-06-148
Page No. 3
Step One: Is Mr. Yarris a o'public official?r,
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(sections87100,87ll3;regulation18700(bxl).) A'publicofficial"is"everymember,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local goveflrment agency. . . .,,
(Section 82048.) As a member of the Healthcare District Board, Mr. Yarris is a public
official within the meaning of the Act.
Step Two: Would Mr. Yarris be making, participating in making or influencing a
governmental decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contracfual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulati on 18702.L ) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, actingwithin the authority of his or her
position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates,
advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3.) As a member of the Healthcare District Board, Mr. Yarris would
be making,participating in making, andlor influencing governmental decisions when
considering decisions related to the Health Education Center.
Step Three: What are Mr. Yarris's 66economic interests?r,
There are six kinds of economic interests recognized under the Act. Those that
may be applicable, according to your account of the facts, are the following:
Business Entity - A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in
which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more, or in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(a) and (d); regulation 18703.1(a) and (b).) However, onlybusinesses
operated for profit are defined by the Act as business entities. (Section 32005.) Under
your account of the facts, the College Authority is a nonprofit organization and the
College District is a government entity. Accordingly, Mr. Ya:ris does not have an
economic interest in either the College Auxiliary or the College District as a business
entity.
Sources of Income -- A public official has an economic interest in any source of
income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within l2 months
prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3.) The facts you have
presented indicate that Mr. Yarris is an employee of the College Auxiliary and receives


File No. I-06-148
Page No. 4
income from the College Auxiliary. If Mr. Yarris receives income from the College
Auxiliary aggregatingto $500 or more within 12 months prior to a decision, Mr. yarris
has an economic interest in the college Auxiliary as a source of income.3
Based upon the facts you have provided, the College Auxiliary is the source of
Mr. Yarris's salary and the College Auxiliary appears to be a source of income to Mr.
Yarris. Moreover, in some circumstances, we have advised that there may be more than a
single source for apaymxrt. (Dorsey, Adviceletter, No. 4-87-176.) Considering the
factthat Mr. Yarris teaches at Grossmont College two to three nights a weeko there is a
question of whether Mr. Yarris's salary could be attributed to the College District and/or
Grossmont College in addition to the College Auxiliary. While you have not provided
sufficient facts to determine whether the College District and/or the Grossmont CoU"g"
could also be considered a source of Mr. Yarris's salary, section 82030(b)e) provides
that salary received from a state, local, or federal agency is not considered income under
the Act. Accordingly, while the College Auxiliary appears to be a source of income to
Mr. Yarris, the College District and Grossmont College are not considered sources of
income to Mr. Yarris, as any salary that could be attributed to the College Dishict and
Grossmont College, which are both govemmental agencies, would not be considered
income under the Act.
Personal Financial Effects -- A public official has an economic interest in his or
her personal finances. In particular, a governmental decision has a personal financial
effect on a public official if the decision will result in the personal op"rrs"r, income,
assets, or liabilities of the official increasing or decreasing. (Section877}3;regulation
18703.s.)
Step Four: Are Mr. Yarris's economic interests directly or indirectly involved in
the decision?
Sources of Income:
After identifying all economic interests, it is necessary to determine whether each
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision. Regulation lr7}a.\a) states
that a source of income is directly involved in a decision before the official,s agency
when that source of income, directly or by an agent:
3 We are not analyzingwhether the College Auxiliary could be considered a local governmental
entity based upon the factors provided in the Commission's opinionlz re Siegel (1977) 3 Fp--pC Ops. 62.
(See also,Flearey Advice Letter, No. 4-01-251; Stone Advice Letter, No. a-S7-Oi 0; and, Francrs Advice
Leller, No. A-86-214.) While we note that a salary received from a govemmental entity is not considered
income under the act (section 82030(b)(2), you have not provided the facts necessary io make a
determination of whether the College Auxiliary should be considered a local governmental agency and
have provided only that the College Auxiliary is a nonprofit organization. Todetermine whether
Mr. Yarris's has a disqualiffing conflict of interest, Mr. Yarris should consider the salary received from the
College Auxiliary income and the College Auxiliary a source of income unless additional facts establish
that the college Auxiliary should be considered a locar govemmental entity.


File No. I-06-148
Page No. 5
"(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency. A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."
Because you have not identified a specific govemmental decision and have only
generally asked whether Mr. Yarris can make, participate in making, or influence the
process and decisions related to the Health Education Center, we cannot determine
whether the College Auxiliary is directly involved in any specific govemmental decision.
If you need additional assistance determining whether Mr. Yarris's economic interest in
the College Auxiliary is directly or indirectly involved in any specific governmental
decision, it is advisable that you seek further assistance, providing all relevant facts.
Personal Financial Effects:
If facts suggest any financial effect on Mr. Ya:ris's personal finances,
Mr. Yarris's economic interest in his personal finances is deemed to be directly involved
in the govemmental decision. (Regulation 18704.5.)
Steps Five and Six: WiII there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on Mr. Yarris's economic interest(s)?
Materiality
Sources of Income
Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify
the materiality standard appropriate to each economic interest.
For economic interests in nonprofit organizations, directly involved in a decision,
which are sources of income to an official, the materiality standard is given at regulation
18705.3(a) which states, "Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is
a source of income to a public official, and who is directly involved in a decision before
the official's agency, is deemed material."
For economic interests in nonprofit organizations, indirectly involved in a
decision, which are sources of income to an official, the materiality standard is given at
regulation 18705.3(b) (copy enclosed). The materiality thresholds in this regulation vary
with the size of the nonprofit organization. Because you have not provided sufficient


File No. I-06-148
Page No. 6
information for us to calculate the materiality threshold for Mr. yarris,s economic
interest in the College Auxiliary, we must leave the calculation up to Mr. yarris.
Mr' Yarris should consult 18705.3(b) to ensure that he identifies the materiality threshold
actually appropriate to the College Auxiliary.
In addition, there is a separate and distinct materiality standard that applies in cases
where there is a "nexus" between duties owed to a source of income and to tire official,s
public agency. The materiality threshold is understandably much lower when a public
official is paid to accomplish some action that is within the official's public decision-
making authority. Under the nexus rule, "Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a
person who is a source of income to a public official is deemed material if the public
official receives or is promised the income to achieve a goal or pulpose which would be
achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision.,, (Regulation 1g705.3(c).)
The rationale for the nexus test is that when an employee earns a salary to
accomplish a purpose that may be advanced by what he oishl does as a pub6t official, we
presume that the employer is benefiting from the actions of the employee in his or her
official capacity. (Yarnell Advice Letter,No. 4-00-161.) Tpicaliy, a..nexus,, is found in
situations where the official is also a high-level employee with direct influence and control
over his or her employer's management or policy decisions. (MoserAdvice Letter, No.
A-03-147; Low Advice Letter, No. 4-99-304.)
You stated that Mr. Yarris is the College Auxiliary's Program Director for the
Occupations Resource Center. In this capacity, Mr. Yarris provides leadership and
direction to regional community colleges in workforce development, is involvld in the
administration and management of all grants and budgets for the College Auxiliary, and
teaches two to three nights a week at Grossmont College. As a Program Director,
Mr' Ya:ris's tasks may include promoting governmental action, which would help to carry
out the College Authority's mission. Thus, a nexus may exist, and a material financial
effect may occur, if apatticular governmental decision in which a high-level administrator
like Mr. Yarris is asked to participate would also achieve, defeat, aid or hinder a policy
pu{pose, or goal of College Auxiliary. (See Moser, supra; RankinAdvice Letter, No. A-
94-310.)
You have not presented enough facts for us to analyze fully whether the nexus test
is met. Accordingly, Mr. Yarris must consider whether the decisions relating to the Health
Education Center will achieve a goal or pu{pose, which will have u r.*o.rub-ly foreseeable
financial effect on the College Auxiliary which he is paid to bring about. If a nexus is
found, any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the College Auxiliary from a
goveflrmental decision relating to the Health Education Center, even a penny,s worth, is
deemed material.


File No. I-06-148
Page No. 7
Personal Financial Effects :
Effects on an official's personal finances arcmateial as stated in regulation
187 05 .5 , which provides in pertinen t pari:
"Pfi T:,::Hffi i:::T jlH:if fi :::ffi fl lTt;
in any l2_monthperiod.,,
Foreseeability

of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining *rr"trr".itlili"uronubly

effect on an economic
interest is '?easonabry foreseeable,lif it i. ,rb;;;i-;ily Iikely that one or more of the
mateiality standards will be met as a resultof th";;;;;ental decision. (Regulation
18706(a)') An effect need not be certain to be coniid"ii *r"u.o nabryforeseeablq,,but it
must be more than a merepossib,ity. (In re rn;*rig75) 1 Fppc ops. 19g.)
ultimately' whether amaterialfinancial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts u"a "it"r-.tances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, supra,
at 198') Because the commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (In
re oglesby (1975),1 FPPC ops. 71), trr" ror".""uiurr#"particurar financial effect is a

that must be leh, in #ost instan""r, i"Trr"lnformed judgment of the public
Because you have not identified a specific govemmental decision norprovided
any facts regarding the foreseeability of ttre potentf,i nrr*"ial effects * tt " cott"g"
Auxiliary or Mr' Yarris's personal dr*""r, we cannot determine whether there will be a
reasonably foreseeable material financial eifect "; M;. ilrrir,. economic interests.
However' if Mr' Yarris determines ttrat trrere i. ";;;ly 
foreseeable financial effect
on one or more of his economic interests with respect to a specific governmental
decision, he may uplty tlematenalitf standard, u, ,"t rortt above. Should you need
further assistance determining iru dirqu-ulifying rooni-", orinterest exists, we suggest that
#"ffi;l;if::"'.fflassistance, 
p'o,,iaing utr ."rl"*ia"ts as they relate to a specific

sovernmentar decision come within any exception
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
govemmental decision affects the public official's e"o;;;i" interests in a manner that is
ll**Uffiil?.Hil:;1ffi i"jli'ichthed;';il;;nur."itr,.;;;ii;;",erary


File No. I-06-t48
Page No. 8
Additionally, in cettun rare circumstances, a public_ official may be called upon to
takepart in a decision despite the fact llrl ft: "ffi;i;i;;; have adisquarifuing conflict of
interest under the "legally required participatro" "**piiJn. This exception applies only in
certain very specific circumstances whereJh. gor"--^"n t agencywould be paralyzed, from
acting. (Section 87101 regulation 1g70g.)
You have not presented any facts indicatingthatthe .,public generally,, or the
"Tegally required participation" exleptions are appticable to Mr. yariis,s ciriumstances,
so we will not address them further.
If you have other questions on this matter,please contact me at (g16) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Le gal Division

Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit Mr. Yarris from making,
participating in making, or influencing Healthcare District decisions related to a Health
Education Center, which may be constructed by the Grossmont-Cuyamaca Community
College District ("College District'), considering his emplo5rment with the Grossmont-
Cuyamaca Community College District Auxiliary Or ganiz ation ("College Auxiliary,,)?
sections.conclusion:
Mr. Yarris will have a conflict of interest disqualifizing him from making,
participating in making, or influencing Healthcare District decisions related to the Health
Education Center if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
I Government Code sections 8 1000 - gl}14. ; rggulation 18329(c)(3).)
rrygl
effect on the College Auxiliary, if the nexus test is met, or if there is a personal financial
effect on Mr. Yarris as discussed herein.

for nursing and other healthcare-related education programs.
sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from makin g, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental deJision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest, in a

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(sections87100,87ll3;regulation18700(bxl).) A'publicofficial"is"everymember,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local goveflrment agency. . . .,,
(Section 82048.) As a member of the Healthcare District Board, Mr. Yarris is a public

commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contracfual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulati on 18702.L ) A public official

position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates,

decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official

considering decisions related to the Health Education Center.

There are six kinds of economic interests recognized under the Act. Those that
may be applicable, according to your account of the facts, are the following:
Business Entity - A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in
which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more, or in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(a) and (d); regulation 18703.1(a) and (b).) However, onlybusinesses
operated for profit are defined by the Act as business entities. (Section 32005.) Under

College District is a government entity. Accordingly, Mr. Ya:ris does not have an
economic interest in either the College Auxiliary or the College District as a business
entity.

income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within l2 months

presented indicate that Mr. Yarris is an employee of the College Auxiliary and receives

Auxiliary aggregatingto $500 or more within 12 months prior to a decision, Mr. yarris
has an economic interest in the college Auxiliary as a source of income.3

Mr. Yarris's salary and the College Auxiliary appears to be a source of income to Mr.
Yarris. Moreover, in some circumstances, we have advised that there may be more than a
single source for apaymxrt. (Dorsey, Adviceletter, No. 4-87-176.) Considering the
factthat Mr. Yarris teaches at Grossmont College two to three nights a weeko there is a
question of whether Mr. Yarris's salary could be attributed to the College District and/or
Grossmont College in addition to the College Auxiliary. While you have not provided
sufficient facts to determine whether the College District and/or the Grossmont CoU"g"
could also be considered a source of Mr. Yarris's salary, section 82030(b)e) provides

the Act. Accordingly, while the College Auxiliary appears to be a source of income to

income to Mr. Yarris, as any salary that could be attributed to the College Dishict and

her personal finances. In particular, a governmental decision has a personal financial
effect on a public official if the decision will result in the personal op"rrs"r, income,
assets, or liabilities of the official increasing or decreasing. (Section877}3;regulation
18703.s.)
Step Four: Are Mr. Yarris's economic interests directly or indirectly involved in
the decision?
Sources of Income:
After identifying all economic interests, it is necessary to determine whether each
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision. Regulation lr7}a.\a) states
that a source of income is directly involved in a decision before the official,s agency
when that source of income, directly or by an agent:
3 We are not analyzingwhether the College Auxiliary could be considered a local governmental
entity based upon the factors provided in the Commission's opinionlz re Siegel (1977) 3 Fp--pC Ops. 62.
(See also,Flearey Advice Letter, No. 4-01-251; Stone Advice Letter, No. a-S7-Oi 0; and, Francrs Advice
Leller, No. A-86-214.) While we note that a salary received from a govemmental entity is not considered
income under the act (section 82030(b)(2), you have not provided the facts necessary io make a
determination of whether the College Auxiliary should be considered a local governmental agency and
have provided only that the College Auxiliary is a nonprofit organization. Todetermine whether
Mr. Yarris's has a disqualiffing conflict of interest, Mr. Yarris should consider the salary received from the
College Auxiliary income and the College Auxiliary a source of income unless additional facts establish
that the college Auxiliary should be considered a locar govemmental entity.
"(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency. A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."
Because you have not identified a specific govemmental decision and have only
generally asked whether Mr. Yarris can make, participate in making, or influence the
process and decisions related to the Health Education Center, we cannot determine
whether the College Auxiliary is directly involved in any specific govemmental decision.
If you need additional assistance determining whether Mr. Yarris's economic interest in
the College Auxiliary is directly or indirectly involved in any specific governmental
decision, it is advisable that you seek further assistance, providing all relevant facts.
Personal Financial Effects:
If facts suggest any financial effect on Mr. Ya:ris's personal finances,
Mr. Yarris's economic interest in his personal finances is deemed to be directly involved
in the govemmental decision. (Regulation 18704.5.)
Steps Five and Six: WiII there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on Mr. Yarris's economic interest(s)?
Materiality
Sources of Income
Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify
the materiality standard appropriate to each economic interest.
For economic interests in nonprofit organizations, directly involved in a decision,
which are sources of income to an official, the materiality standard is given at regulation
18705.3(a) which states, "Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is
a source of income to a public official, and who is directly involved in a decision before
the official's agency, is deemed material."
For economic interests in nonprofit organizations, indirectly involved in a
decision, which are sources of income to an official, the materiality standard is given at
regulation 18705.3(b) (copy enclosed). The materiality thresholds in this regulation vary
with the size of the nonprofit organization. Because you have not provided sufficient
information for us to calculate the materiality threshold for Mr. yarris,s economic
interest in the College Auxiliary, we must leave the calculation up to Mr. yarris.
Mr' Yarris should consult 18705.3(b) to ensure that he identifies the materiality threshold
actually appropriate to the College Auxiliary.
In addition, there is a separate and distinct materiality standard that applies in cases
where there is a "nexus" between duties owed to a source of income and to tire official,s
public agency. The materiality threshold is understandably much lower when a public
official is paid to accomplish some action that is within the official's public decision-
making authority. Under the nexus rule, "Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a
person who is a source of income to a public official is deemed material if the public
official receives or is promised the income to achieve a goal or pulpose which would be
achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision.,, (Regulation 1g705.3(c).)
The rationale for the nexus test is that when an employee earns a salary to
accomplish a purpose that may be advanced by what he oishl does as a pub6t official, we
presume that the employer is benefiting from the actions of the employee in his or her
official capacity. (Yarnell Advice Letter,No. 4-00-161.) Tpicaliy, a..nexus,, is found in
situations where the official is also a high-level employee with direct influence and control
over his or her employer's management or policy decisions. (MoserAdvice Letter, No.
A-03-147; Low Advice Letter, No. 4-99-304.)
You stated that Mr. Yarris is the College Auxiliary's Program Director for the
Occupations Resource Center. In this capacity, Mr. Yarris provides leadership and
direction to regional community colleges in workforce development, is involvld in the
administration and management of all grants and budgets for the College Auxiliary, and
teaches two to three nights a week at Grossmont College. As a Program Director,
Mr' Ya:ris's tasks may include promoting governmental action, which would help to carry
out the College Authority's mission. Thus, a nexus may exist, and a material financial
effect may occur, if apatticular governmental decision in which a high-level administrator
like Mr. Yarris is asked to participate would also achieve, defeat, aid or hinder a policy
pu{pose, or goal of College Auxiliary. (See Moser, supra; RankinAdvice Letter, No. A-
94-310.)
You have not presented enough facts for us to analyze fully whether the nexus test
is met. Accordingly, Mr. Yarris must consider whether the decisions relating to the Health
Education Center will achieve a goal or pu{pose, which will have u r.*o.rub-ly foreseeable
financial effect on the College Auxiliary which he is paid to bring about. If a nexus is
found, any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the College Auxiliary from a
goveflrmental decision relating to the Health Education Center, even a penny,s worth, is
deemed material.

QUESTION: Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit Mr. Yarris from making,
participating in making, or influencing Healthcare District decisions related to a Health
Education Center, which may be constructed by the Grossmont-Cuyamaca Community
College District ("College District'), considering his emplo5rment with the Grossmont-
Cuyamaca Community College District Auxiliary Or ganiz ation ("College Auxiliary,,)?

CONCLUSION: Mr. Yarris will have a conflict of interest disqualifizing him from making,
participating in making, or influencing Healthcare District decisions related to the Health
Education Center if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
I Government Code sections 8 1000 - gl}14. ; rggulation 18329(c)(3).)

effect on the College Auxiliary, if the nexus test is met, or if there is a personal financial
effect on Mr. Yarris as discussed herein.

Scott General Counsel Grossmont Healthcare District 16935 West Bernardo Drive, Suite 170 San Diego, CA 92127 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-06-148 Dear Mr. Scott: This letter is in response to your request for advice, on behalf of Mr. Robert p. Yarris a board member of the Grossmont Healthcare District ("Healthcare District,), regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the ,,Act,).i Because your request seeks general guidance beyond a specific goverrmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.t QUESTION Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit Mr. Yarris from making, participating in making, or influencing Healthcare District decisions related to a Health Education Center, which may be constructed by the Grossmont-Cuyamaca Community College District ("College District'), considering his emplo5rment with the Grossmont- Cuyamaca Community College District Auxiliary Or ganiz ation ("College Auxiliary,,)? CONCLUSION Mr. Yarris will have a conflict of interest disqualifizing him from making, participating in making, or influencing Healthcare District decisions related to the Health Education Center if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial I Government Code sections 8 1000 - gl}14. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not p.oiria" the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; rggulation 18329(c)(3).) rrygl File No. I-06-148 Page No. 2 effect on the College Auxiliary, if the nexus test is met, or if there is a personal financial effect on Mr. Yarris as discussed herein. FACTS The Healthcare District is located in La Mesa, Califomia and is organizedunder the provisions of the Health and Safety Code sections 32000 et seq., to provide healthcare services and to operate healthcare related facilities for residents iniast San Diego County. Mr. Yarris has served on the Healthcare District Board since December 1992. In addition to his Healthcare District Board position, Mr. Yarris is also an employee of the College Auxiliary, holding a position as the Program Director of the Regional'Health Occupations Resource Center. As an employee of the College Auxiliary, Mr. yarris teaches a class at Grossmont College two to three times a week and provides leadership and direction to regional community colleges in workforce development. In addition, Mr. Yarris's work involves the administration and management of all grants and budgets for the College Auxiliary. The College Auxiliary is a nonprofit public corporation, which receives grants from private foundations and contracts with the College District. As an employee of the College Auxiliary, Mr. Yarris receives no salary or other form of remuneration from the College District. The College District and the Grossmont College, which is a college of the College District, are exploring the construction of a Health Education Center on property o*r"d by the Healthcare District, which may also include funding from the UeAtfrcare District for nursing and other
